coredata.dc:title: Cefminox sodium carbon nanodots for treatment and bacterial detection of bloodstream infection
coredata.dc:creator: Kuang, Yulan
Song, Meiwei
Zhou, Xuechun
Mi, Jiqiang
Zhang, Zhe
Liu, Guancheng
Shen, Zhenru
Liu, Zhenquan
Chen, Changbao
Wu, Mei X.
Zhao, Yu
Yang, Bai
Jiang, Yingnan
coredata.dc:description: Cefminox sodium carbon nanodots were synthesized via a rapid one-step microwave method using cefminox sodium as sole precursor. The prepared cefminox sodium carbon nanodots had good water solubility and the diameter was concentrated at 1.29 ± 0.40 nm with relative uniform. They exhibited fluorescence excitation-dependent property, of which optimal excitation wavelength was ∼ 390 nm, and optimal emission wavelength was ∼ 465 nm. As retained part of the structure and antibacterial properties of cefminox sodium, cefminox sodium carbon nanodots had low biological toxicity and sufficient antibacterial activity against bacteria in vitro. In vivo experiments demonstrated cefminox sodium carbon nanodots could exert a good therapeutic effect on acute bloodstream infection. In the mixed solution (Escherichia coli, blood and cefminox sodium carbon nanodots), the fluorescent intensity exhibited quadratic functional relationship with the concentration of Escherichia coli. The detection concentration range for Escherichia coli was 0.5 × 106 ∼ 1 × 109 CFU/mL, and the detection limit was 3.7 × 105 CFU/mL. It suggested cefminox sodium carbon nanodots can rapidly and quantitatively detect the number of Escherichia coli in blood while effectively treating bloodstream infection. Due to the long period of clinical detection time (about 1–5 days or more) for bacterial counts in the blood of patients, this method provides a new strategy for rapid clinical treatment and effective monitoring of the degree of infection in patients.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cefminox sodium
Carbon nanodots
Escherichia coli
Acute bloodstream infection
Fluorescent quantitation of bacteria

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of the distribution of CS-CDs in bacteria by TEM and SEM
Detection of minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and evaluation of bacterial resistance to CS-CDs
Testing in vitro cytotoxicity of CS-CDs
In vivo study on the treatment of acute E. Coli BSI by CS-CDs
Quantitation of bacteria in mice blood

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Jilin Provincial Scientific and Technological Development Program

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and size distribution diagram of prepared CS-CDs; (b) Normalized fluorescence spectra of CS-CDs under different excitation wavelengths ranging from 310 nm to 490 nm; (c) Excitation-Emission-Matrix spectra of CS-CDs; (d) Fluorescence attenuation of CS-CDs; (e) UV–vis absorption spectra of CS and CS-CDs in aqueous solution. Inset: photographs of CS-CDs solution under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right); (f) FT-IR spectra of CS and CS-CDs; (g) XPS spectra of CS-CDs: High-resolution XPS C 1 s spectrum (h); O 1 s spectrum (i); N 1 s spectrum (j); S 2p spectrum (k); Na 1 s spectrum (l) of CS-CDs.

(a) Cell viability of MC3T3-E1, LO2 and HEK 293 T cells incubated with CS-CDs at different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 150, and 200 μg/mL) for 48 h; (b) Photos of antibacterial plate experiments of E. coli with different concentrations of CS-CDs (1, 3, 5, 10, and 20 mg/mL), where the scale bar was 2 cm; (c) Corresponding diagram of diameters of bacteriostatic zone in the bacteriostatic plate at different concentrations of CS-CDs; (d) Diagram of OD value of LB medium, Control (E. coli solution), E. coli cocultured with CS (50 μg/mL), and E. coli cultured with CS-CDs at different concentrations (10, 20, 30, and 50 μg/mL), from 0 to 12 h; (e) The sensitivity curves of E. coli to CS (black line) and CS-CDs (red line) for 15 passages.

(a1-a5) TEM images of E. coli treated with 1 mg/mL CS-CDs for different time (0, 2, 6, 12, and 24 h), where the scale bars were 500 nm; (b1-b5) TEM images of E. coli treated with 1 mg/mL CS for different time (0, 2, 6, 12, and 24 h), where the scale bars were 500 nm; (c1-c5) SEM images of E. coli treated with 1 mg/mL CS-CDs for different time (0, 2, 6, 12, and 24 h), where the scale bars were 500 nm; (d1-d5) SEM images of E. coli treated with 1 mg/mL CS for different time (0, 2, 6, 12, and 24 h), where the scale bars were 500 nm; (e) The biofilm biomass of E. coli cultured with CS-CDs at different concentrations (0, 20, 30, 50, 80, 100, 150, and 200 μg/mL); (f) The biofilm biomass of E. coli cultured with CS at different concentrations (0, 20, 30, 50, 80, 100, 150, and 200 μg/mL).

(a) Photos of bacteria counting plates of mice in Model group at 1st d, CS group and CS-CDs group at 1st d, 3rd d, 5th d, and 7th d (n = 3), where the scale bar was 2 cm; (b) The corresponding bacteria count plate statistic diagram of mice in Model group at 1st d, CS group and CS-CDs group at 1st d, 3rd d, 5th d, and 7th d (n = 5); (c) Statistic diagram of serum IL-6 levels of mice in Model group, Control group, Control + CS-CDs group, CS group, and CS-CDs group at 4 h in the 1st day and 7th d (n = 5); (d) The statistic diagram of white blood cell count of mice in Control group, Control + CS-CDs group, CS group and CS-CDs group at 1, 4 (inset), 48, 96 and 144 h (n = 5).

(a) Image of the same blood solution with increasing concentrations of E. coli (0.5 × 106, 2.0 × 106, 5.0 × 106, 1.0 × 107, 1.0 × 108, 2.0 × 108, 5.0 × 108, and 1.0 × 109 CFU/mL), incubated with 1 mg/mL antibacterial CS-CDs for 6 h under bright light and UV light of 365 nm; (b) The corresponding calculated fitting curve of fluorescence intensity (Ex = 390 nm, Em = 465 nm) to the increasing concentrations of E. coli for different time (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 h); (Table 1) The corresponding fitting curve equation of fluorescence intensity with the increasing concentration of E. coli.
Synthetic route of CS-CDs and the application for BSI treatment, as well as fluorescent quantitative detection of bacteria concentration.
Fitting curve equation of fluorescence intensity with the increasing number of E. coli.

head.ce:title: Cefminox sodium carbon nanodots for treatment and bacterial detection of bloodstream infection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jilin Ginseng Academy, Changchun University of Chinese Medicine, Changchun 130117, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Wellman Center for Photomedicines, Massachusetts General Brigham, Department of Dermatology, Harvard Medical School, Boston, MA 02114, U.S

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Jilin Ginseng Academy, Changchun University of Chinese Medicine, Changchun 130117, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jilin Ginseng Academy, Changchun University of Chinese Medicine

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed to the work equally and should be regarded as co-first author.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cefminox sodium carbon nanodots were synthesized via a rapid one-step microwave method using cefminox sodium as sole precursor. The prepared cefminox sodium carbon nanodots had good water solubility and the diameter was concentrated at 1.29 ± 0.40 nm with relative uniform. They exhibited fluorescence excitation-dependent property, of which optimal excitation wavelength was ∼ 390 nm, and optimal emission wavelength was ∼ 465 nm. As retained part of the structure and antibacterial properties of cefminox sodium, cefminox sodium carbon nanodots had low biological toxicity and sufficient antibacterial activity against bacteria in vitro. In vivo experiments demonstrated cefminox sodium carbon nanodots could exert a good therapeutic effect on acute bloodstream infection. In the mixed solution (Escherichia coli, blood and cefminox sodium carbon nanodots), the fluorescent intensity exhibited quadratic functional relationship with the concentration of Escherichia coli. The detection concentration range for Escherichia coli was 0.5 × 106 ∼ 1 × 109 CFU/mL, and the detection limit was 3.7 × 105 CFU/mL. It suggested cefminox sodium carbon nanodots can rapidly and quantitatively detect the number of Escherichia coli in blood while effectively treating bloodstream infection. Due to the long period of clinical detection time (about 1–5 days or more) for bacterial counts in the blood of patients, this method provides a new strategy for rapid clinical treatment and effective monitoring of the degree of infection in patients.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bloodstream infection (BSI) is a systemic infectious disease caused by various pathogenic microorganisms such as Escherichia coli (E. coli), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), Klebsiella pneumoniae (K. pneumoniae), and Candida albicans (C. albicans) invading the blood fiercely. It is one of the common clinical infections [1,2]. With the extensive development of traumatic diagnosis and treatment techniques, and the widespread application of broad-spectrum antibiotics and hormones, the incidence of BSI is increasing dramatically year by year. BSI develops rapidly and has a high mortality rate, that approximately 8 million people die each year because of BSI worldwide. Clinical etiological diagnosis of BSI often depends on multiple positive blood cultures obtained from different body parts. Although blood culture is the current standard for diagnosing BSI, the positive detection rate of blood culture is low. Generally, the detection time of blood culture is too long (about 1–5 days or more), which cannot timely meet the needs of early clinical, rapid diagnosis, and treatment in time. According to statistics, for every hour of delay in treatment the mortality rate increases by about 7.6%, and for every 6 h of delay in treatment, the mortality rate increases by about 58% [3–5]. Obviously, early and rapid diagnosis of BSI with timely drug treatment can significantly increase the survival rate of acute BSI patients.

Antibiotics are the most widely used drugs for antibacterial therapy (including acute BSI) in clinical practice. They mainly act on the growth process of bacteria (such as inhibiting cell wall synthesis and interfering the synthesis of important proteins, DNA and RNA). With the extensive use of antibiotics, bacteria gradually produce inactivating enzymes or new target proteins (antibiotics cannot bind to the original target proteins on the bacterial membrane), and their drug resistance is increasing fast. The development of new antibacterial drugs and new therapeutic alternative strategies has become an urgent clinical need [6,7]. Nowadays, antibacterial nano drugs have received extensive attention due to their unique size, microscopic properties, bacteriostatic activity, and lower bacterial resistance [8,9]. Common antibacterial nano drugs include antibiotic-loaded nanoparticles, metal/metal oxide nanoparticles, graphene/graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, and organic–inorganic self-assembled nanoparticles [10–20]. However, considering the problems of nanomaterials components, biocompatibility, and biotoxicity, the reported antibacterial nanomaterials are mostly used for in vitro wound antibacterial therapy. Clinical application of many antibacterial nanomedicine materials is greatly limited under the premise of low toxicity in vivo.

Carbon nanodots (CDs) with unique fluorescence properties have been widely used in disease treatment, drug delivery, bioimaging and biosensor in the past decade, due to their wide preparation sources, low cost, environmental friendliness, high biocompatibility and low biological toxicity [10,21–32]. Some literatures have reported the synthesis of CDs from antibiotics and studied their application in the field of antibacterial therapy [27–32]. In 2016, Chandra et al. reported CDs based on diammonium hydrogen citrate amikacin, which could selectively detect E. coli in vitro by their fluorescence changes [27]. Gao et al. synthesized CDs based on ampicillin. Antibacterial activity of the CDs was similar to that of original ampicillin in vitro [30]. In 2021, Liang's research group used levofloxacin hydrochloride to synthesize CDs via hydrothermal method. It also showed a similar broad-spectrum antibacterial effect to original levofloxacin in vitro. The authors claimed that CDs retained part of the antibacterial activity of the pre-antibiotics [31]. That is to say, based on a large number of antibiotics, a variety of CDs with antibacterial activity can be prepared to treat bacterial diseases. However, most of these studies as well as other antibacterial nanomaterials still remained in the wounds healing and detection in vitro. For the treatment of antibacterial diseases in vivo (such as BSI), the clinical application research of antibacterial CDs has not been developed.

As a common clinical antibiotic, cefminox sodium (CS) is a kind of cephamycin derivative, of which active properties are similar to the third-generation cephalosporins. It shows strong and broad-spectrum antibacterial effects on E. coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae (K. pneumoniae), Haemophilus influenzae (H. influenzae) and Proteus spp., weak inhibitory effect on Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and little inhibitory effect on Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa). CS is often used for infection of tonsils, respiratory tract, urinary tract, abdominal cavity, as well as BSI. Here, in order to further expand the clinical applications of antibiotic-based CDs, CS was used as the only reaction material to synthesize cefminox sodium carbon nanodots (CS-CDs) via a rapid microwave method (Scheme 1 ). The size of CS-CDs was centered at 1.29 ± 0.40 nm with typical excitation-dependent properties. As CS-CDs retained a part of the CS structure after the reaction, they possessed good water solubility, antibacterial activity and low biological toxicity. Bacteria can rapidly lyse in CS-CDs solution within a short period of time, and their bacteria resistance is lower than that of CS. The results of in vivo experiments showed CS-CDs could effectively treat acute BSI of E. coli. At the same time, based on the fluorescence sensing properties, CS-CDs can quickly detect (∼6 h) the concentration of E. coli in blood. The detection concentration range for E. coli was 0.5 × 106 ∼ 1 × 109 CFU/mL, and the detection limit (LOD) was 3.7 × 105 CFU/mL. This will greatly help to improve the speed of diagnosis and treatment of acute BSI, as well as the survival rate of patients. In this study, an integrated system for fluorescence diagnosis and treatment of CS-CDs for BSI was constructed. It provides a new strategy for clinical diagnosis and treatment of acute BSI.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CS-CDs were obtained using a Tecnai F20 electron microscope (FEI Company, Netherlands). Sigma 300 electron microscope (ZEISS, Germany) was used to obtain scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of CS-CDs incubated with E. coli. The Shimadzu UV-2550 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer was used to obtain ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra. The Hitachi F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to obtain fluorescence spectra. Infrared spectra were obtained by a Vector 33 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The FLS 920 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, Scotland) was used to obtain the fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was conducted on an ESCALAB 250 X photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). For the 13C NMR method, a Bruker TM 500 spectrometer was used (Bruker, Switzerland).

The CS (>92%) was purchased from Hubei Weideli Chemical Science and Technology Co., Ltd. (China). Cell counting kit-8 was purchased from Wuhan Boster Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (China). Anti-fluorescence attenuation mounting tablets were purchased from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. Dulbecco’s modified eagle medium (DMEM), roswell park memorial institute medium (RPMI 1640), penicillin–streptomycin solution, phosphate buffered saline (PBS), and certified fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from VivaCell, Shanghai, China. The Luria broth (LB) medium was purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). The 1.0% crystal violet was obtained from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd (China). The water used in all experiments was ultrapure water.

CS-CDs was synthesized by one-step microwave method. First, 25 mL CS solution (1 mg/mL) was transferred to a 100 mL beaker and heated for 10 min in a household microwave oven at 360, 540, 720 and 900 W. After reaction, the solution was coarsely filtered with 0.22 μm polyethersulfone membrane to remove larger products. The filtered solution was then added into an activated ultrafiltration tube (3000 MWCO) and centrifuged for two times (5000 rpm, 15 min). Finally, the yellow CS-CDs solution was obtained and stored in a refrigerator at 4℃.

The E. coli (Gram-negative bacteria, facultative anaerobic, ATCC25922) was transferred from Luria-Bertani (LB) solid medium to LB liquid medium and cultured at 37℃ for 8 h at 200 rpm, to reach logarithmic growth stage. K. pneumoniae (Gram-negative bacteria, aerobic and facultative anaerobic, ATCC13883), S. aureus (Gram-positive bacteria, facultative anaerobic, ATCC25923), and P. aeruginosa (Gram-negative bacteria, aerobic, ATCC27853) was cultured the same as E. coli, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the distribution of CS-CDs in bacteria by TEM and SEM
ce:section.ce:para: The E. coli in logarithmic growth phase was incubated with the same concentration (1 mg/mL) of CS-CDs for 0, 2, 6, 12 and 24 h, respectively. After centrifugation, fixation, dehydration, coating, and section staining, the E. coli was imaged by TEM [38].

8 mL overnight cultured bacterial solutions with 1 mg/mL of CS-CDs were cocultured. Bacteria was collected after centrifugation (4000 rpm, 5 min) at 0, 2, 6, 12, and 24 h, respectively. Then, each bacteria sample was fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde for 4 h, washed three times with phosphate buffer, fixed with 1% osmic acid and washed with distilled water three times. After dehydration of gradient ethanol, each sample was dried at the critical point and sprayed gold. Last, the SEM observation of bacteria samples were carried out.

All cells were from an American-type culture collection (ATCC, Manassas, VA, US). The mouse embryo osteoblast precursor cells (MC3T3-E1) and Human embryonic kidneys 293 T cells (HEK 293 T) were cultured in DMEM medium. Human normal liver cells (LO2) were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium. All cells were cultured in a humidified incubator containing 5% CO2 at 37°C.

First, filter paper with a diameter of 6 mm was placed in different concentrations of CS-CDs solution and soaked overnight. Then, E. coli was coated on LB solid medium. Finally, stick each soaked filter paper in the middle of the culture plate. The plates were incubated overnight in a constant temperature and humidity incubator at 37°C. The antibacterial plate experiment of K. pneumoniae, S. aureus, and P. aeruginosa was performed the same as E. coli, respectively.

LB medium, Control (E. coli solution), E. coli cocultured with CS (50 μg/mL), and cocultured with CS-CDs at different concentrations (10 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL, 30 μg/mL and 50 μg/mL) were co-incubated in a 37°C water bath constant temperature shaker. After 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 h, 200 μL solution was aspirated and transferred to a sterile 96-well plate. Then the OD value change of the system was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and evaluation of bacterial resistance to CS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared CS-CDs were diluted with LB medium to obtain CS-CDs with different concentrations (32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1, 0.5, 0.25, 0.125, and 0.0625 μg/mL). In a sterile 96-well plate, 50 μL CS-CDs solution, LB medium and E. coli liquid of 1 × 106 CFU/mL were added to each well and shaken evenly. After incubating at 37°C for 24 h, recorded the MIC value. The concentration of CS-CDs corresponded to the turbid well as the MIC value. Aspirated the bacterial liquid from the well with 1/2 MIC value, spread it on the agar plate, and continued to cultivate. After picking single colony, placed them in LB medium and continued to cultivate them in a shaker at 37°C. The CS group experiment was the same as above [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing in vitro cytotoxicity of CS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Using CS-CDs vs. MC3T3-E1 cells as an example, the MC3T3-E1 cells were cultured to the logarithmic growth phase, with the appropriate cell density of 3000 ∼ 5000 cells/well. Then, MC3T3-E1 cells were transferred and cultured into a 96-well cell culture plate. Afterward, 180 μL cell suspension per well was placed in an incubator containing 5% CO2 at 37°C. When cells were completely attached to the cell bottle wall, 20 μL CS-CDs was added at different concentrations. Next, CS-CDs concentrations with cells in each well were 0, 50, 100, 150, and 200 μg/mL (each concentration had 6 multiple wells). After CS-CDs and MC3T3-E1 cells were incubated for 48 h, 20 μL CCK-8 was added to each well. The 96-well cell culture plate was placed in the incubator for 30 min, and OD value was measured at 450 nm with a microplate reader (Infinite M200 PRO from TECAN, Switzerland). MC3T3-E1 cell viability was calculated according to the OD value.
CCK-8 method was used to detect the viability of the other two cells (LO2, and HEK 293 T) after incubation with CS-CDs, and the experimental procedures were the same as above.

E. coli was used to evaluate the bacterial imaging ability of CS-CDs. E. coli (∼2 × 108 CFU/mL) was mixed with CS-CDs (1 mg/mL) and incubated at 37°C for 0–12 h. E. coli solution was collected by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 5 min, washed three times with PBS buffer, transferred to a slide glass, added 10 μL anti-fluorescence attenuator, and covered the back with a coverslip. Then it was imaged by a fluorescent microscope.

Overnight suspensions of E. coli were diluted to OD600 of 0.2. Then E. coli suspensions were inoculated into 48-well plates (400 μL per well) and incubated at 37℃ for 72 h. During bacterial biofilm culture, half of suspension in each well was replaced with fresh medium every 24 h. After the bacterial biofilm matured, the bacterial biofilm was washed with PBS buffer to remove free bacteria for subsequent experiments.

400 μL CS and CS-CDs at different concentrations (0, 20, 30, 50, 80, 100, 150 and 200 μg/mL) added into the mature E. coli biofilm, respectively. After incubating at 37℃ for 24 h under static conditions, the mixture was washed three times with PBS buffer solution, and fixed with 400 μL methanol for 15 min. Then the mixture was stained with 400 μL 1.0% crystal violet solution for 5 min, washed with PBS to remove dyes, and processed with 400 μL 33.3% acetic acid solution for 30 min to dissolve crystal violet. At last, the absorbance of the samples at 570 nm was measured to quantify the biomass of biofilm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo study on the treatment of acute E. Coli BSI by CS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: All animal experimental procedures were conducted according to all relevant ethical regulations and were approved by the Animal Care & Welfare Committee of the Changchun University of Chinese Medicine. Male ICR mice (6–8 weeks old, weighing about 30 g) were purchased from Liaoning Changsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. E. coli was injected into mice via tail vein, and the dose per mouse was 2 × 108 CFU/mL to establish BSI mice model. The mice were randomly divided into 5 groups (n = 5). The dosage (80 mg/kg) was determined by the body weight of mice, according to clinical treatment methods, once every 24 h. Administration details were as follows: CS-CDs group (the corresponding dose of CS-CDs was injected into tail vein after injecting E. coli); CS group (the corresponding dose of CS was injected into tail vein after injecting E. coli); Model group (the corresponding dose of physiological saline was injected into tail vein after injecting E. coli); Control group (the corresponding dose of normal saline was injected into tail vein); Control + CS-CDs group (the corresponding dose of CS-CDs was injected into tail vein). 1 h after injection of E. coli solution to mice, drug injection was performed. The day of administration was set as the first day of the treatment cycle. The mortality rate of mice in Model group was ∼ 100% within 24 h after injection of E. coli solution. All data of mice in the Model group (including the number of E. coli in the blood of mice, IL-6 indicators, in vivo white blood cells, body temperature and H&E staining) was obtained within 12 h after the establishment of BSI. Body temperature of mice was measured daily. On the 1st, 3rd, 5th, and 7th days, three mice were randomly selected, peripheral blood was collected for blood culture (KK dilution method), and white blood cells were counted. On the first and seventh days after treatment, the serum of randomly selected mice was taken for the determination of IL-6 levels. After 7 days of treatment, all mice were euthanized for anatomical and histopathological analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitation of bacteria in mice blood
ce:section.ce:para: 1 mL E. coli suspension with different concentrations (0.5 × 106, 2.0 × 106, 5.0 × 106, 1.0 × 107, 1.0 × 108, 2.0 × 108, 5.0 × 108, and 1.0 × 109 CFU/mL) were prepared by diluted with normal saline. Secondly, each of the above 8 samples was incubated with 2 mg/mL CS-CDs. Final concentration of CS-CDs in mixed solution was the same of 1 mg/mL. Anticoagulant blood was taken from ICR mice and 6 μL was added to each sample. At 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 h of the incubation, fluorescence spectra of each mixing sample were determined under 390 nm excitation for analysis.
The similar quantitation experiment of K. pneumoniae, S. aureus, P. aeruginosa, E. coli CFT073, and E. coli DH5α in mice blood was performed the same as the above E. coli, respectively.

Note that in microwave means, the reactive microwave power plays an important role in molecular structure changes [33–35]. In order to keep the antibacterial structure and activity of CS molecules as much as possible during the formation of CDs, CDs were prepared with four different reaction powers, 360, 540, 720 and 900 W. Their fluorescence properties and antibacterial activities were preliminarily investigated (Supporting Information, Fig. S1 and Fig. S2). When synthesis power was 540 W, the obtained CS-CDs showed optimal fluorescence properties and antibacterial effects. The diameter of CS-CDs synthesized by 540 W was concentrated at 1.29 ± 0.40 nm, and the size was relatively uniform (Fig. 1 a). CS-CDs exhibited distinct fluorescence excitation-dependent properties (Fig. 1b). At the optimal excitation wavelength of ∼ 390 nm, the optimal emission wavelength of CS-CDs is ∼ 465 nm. Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra showed that there was only one fluorescent emitting substance in the CS-CDs system, which meant the product was single (Fig. 1c). The third-order decay exponential function was fitted according to the fluorescence decay curve of CS-CDs (Fig. 1d). Three fluorescence lifetimes (τ) were obtained, indicating that CS-CDs had three fluorescence centers (Supporting Information, Table S3). Average lifetime of CS-CDs was 3.48 ns, and quantum yield of CS-CDs was 0.65%.

The CS-CDs aqueous solution was yellow and showed bright blue-green fluorescence under the UV light of 365 nm (Fig. 1e). In UV–vis absorption spectrum of CS, absorption peak at 231 nm was due to the formation of a conjugation between the C = C bond of hydrothiazine and the carboxyl substituent on CS molecule, resulting in π → π* transition. With the progress of microwave reaction and the formation of CS-CDs, the carboxyl group of this site underwent intermolecular reaction, and the conjugated structure of hydrothiazine in CS-CDs became more stable. The absorption peak at 231 nm was stronger. For CS molecule, the absorption peak at 273 nm originated from the conjugation between the β-lactam ring and the hydrothiazine ring, resulting in n → π* transition. In the process of microwave heating, the unstable β-lactam ring was hydrolyzed (four-membered ring with high tension and poor stability), and the absorption peak at 273 nm disappeared. For CS-CDs, the S attached to the tetrazole ring containing lone pair electrons which may lead to intermolecular crosslinking during microwave heating. Conjugation degree of the system was enhanced, resulting an absorption at 300 ∼ 400 nm. In IR spectra, both CS and CS-CDs had strong and broad absorption peaks in the range of 3500–3100 cm−1, which were attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H (Fig. 1f). This indicated intermolecular hydrogen bonds existed in both CS and CS-CDs systems before and after the reaction. For both CS and CS-CDs: absorption peak at 1680–1590 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of C = O, C = C, C = N and N = N; absorption peak at 1524 cm−1 was the stretching vibration of C-N of amide group on secondary side chain and in-plane bending vibration of N-H; peaks at 1420–1270 cm−1 were the stretching vibration of C-N and in-plane bending vibration of C-H; peaks at 1210–1010 cm−1 were the stretching vibration of C-O; absorption peak at 810–625 cm−1 originated from the stretching vibration of C-S and the out-of-plane bending vibration of C-H. For CS, absorption peak at 1769 cm−1 originated from the C = O stretching vibration of the β-lactam ring. For CS-CDs, the absorption peak here almost disappeared, which was the same as the result in UV–vis absorption spectrum. The 13C NMR spectra was consistent with the characterization results of the IR spectrum (Supporting Information, Fig. S4).

XPS spectra of CS-CDs showed CS-CDs were composed of five elements, C, O, N, S, and Na, corresponding to the composition of CS (Fig. 1g). High-resolution C 1 s spectrum showed that the CS-CDs surface contained C-C/C = C, C-N, C-O, and C = O (Fig. 1h). The O 1 s spectrum showed C = O and C-O related peaks (Fig. 1i). The N 1 s spectrum had two peaks at 399.2 and 401.1 eV, which belonged to pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively (Fig. 1j). Na 1 s had an absorption peak at 1071.9 eV, indicating that Na was attached to the carboxyl group on the hydrothiazine ring (Fig. 1k). The S 2p spectrum had absorption peaks at 161.7, 163.0, and 164.2 eV, which belonged to S-C, S-H, and S-S, respectively (Fig. 1l). The appearance of the S-S absorption peak may be due to the intermolecular cross-linking of S on the CS side chain during the formation of CS-CDs. It may be due to the mild reaction conditions (short reaction time and low reaction power), many chemical bonds (such as hydrothiazine ring and tetrazole ring) of CS were retained in the CS-CDs system. Thus, CS-CDs may retain part of antibacterial activity of CS.

Based on the fluorescent probe function of CS-CDs, fluorescence images were obtained after incubating of CS-CDs with E. coli for 0–12 h at room temperature (Supporting Information, Fig. S5). Fluorescence images were obtained at 0, 1, 2, 6, 8, and 12 h under excitation of 488 nm wavelength. It can be seen that when the incubation time was 1 h, the system appeared fluorescence, indicating the rapid interaction between CS-CDs and E. coli. With the extension of incubation time, fluorescence of the system gradually increased. At 6–8 h, the fluorescence intensity reached maximum and then gradually decreased, such as at 12 h.

Low cytotoxicity was an important factor for biomedical application of CS-CDs, such as for the treatment of BSI. Here, Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) method was used to detect cytotoxicity of CS-CDs on three normal cells (MC3T3-E1, LO2, and HEK 293 T). After incubating with 200 μg/mL CS-CDs for 48 h, MC3T3-E1, LO2, and HEK 293 T cells showed a proliferation trend (Fig. 2 a). The results proved CS-CDs had low biological toxicity.

In vitro antibacterial tests were performed to explore antibacterial properties of CS-CDs solutions with different concentrations (1, 3, 5, 10, and 20 mg/mL) (Fig. 2b). Diameter of inhibition zone on the agar plate gradually increased with the increase of CS-CDs solution concentration (Fig. 2c).

Meanwhile, we performed the similar antibacterial plate experiments of CS-CDs against K. pneumoniae (Gram-negative bacteria, aerobic and facultative anaerobic), S. aureus (Gram-positive bacteria, facultative anaerobic), and P. aeruginosa (Gram-negative bacteria, aerobic) (Supporting Information, Fig. S6). The experimental results showed that CS-CDs had good antibacterial activity against K. pneumoniae and could also inhibit St. aureus at high concentrations, but displayed little inhibition on P. aeruginosa. It proved that CS-CDs showed a similar antibacterial performance to CS.

OD value of LB medium, Control (E. coli solution), E. coli cultured with CS (50 μg/mL), and E. coli cocultured with CS-CDs at different concentrations (10, 20, 30 and 50 μg/mL) were monitored from 0 to 12 h (Fig. 2d) under the same conditions. OD value of LB medium hardly changed with time. OD value of E. coli suspension rose rapidly to the plateau phase. During 12 h incubation, with the increasing concentration of CS-CDs (from 10 to 50 μg/mL), OD value of the incubated solution decreased significantly. The bacteriostatic effect of CS-CDs at 50 μg/mL was almost the same as CS at 50 μg/mL. It may be due to the mild reaction condition, many chemical bonds of CS were maintained in the CS-CDs system. Combining the previous reports [30,31], it suggested CS-CDs retained the similar antibacterial activity of CS. It is well known, antibacterial mechanisms of CS all act on the bacterial cell walls and promote bacteriolysis [36,37]: The first is that CS can bind to penicillin-binding proteins with a strong affinity, which inhibits the synthesis of bacterial cell walls; The second one is that CS binds to peptidoglycan in the bacterial cell walls, and inhibits peptidoglycan to produce specific lipoprotein of gram-negative bacteria, thereby destroys the bacterial cell walls and promotes bacteriolysis. That is to say, antibacterial mechanism of CS-CDs is also to affects bacteria on their cell walls and then promotes bacteriolysis. In addition, we measured the zeta potential of original CS-CDs and CS-CDs in physiological pH value of blood (∼7.4) (Supporting Information, Fig. S7). The overall charge of CS-CDs was −5.57 mV, and the surface charge of CS-CDs at the physiological pH (∼7.4) was −10.10 mV. Considering the surface of the cell wall of E. coli is also negative charge, there isn’t the antibacterial activity mechanism involved electrostatic interactions between CS-CDs and bacteria.

To investigate the resistance of bacteria to CS-CDs, sub-inhibitory concentrations of E. coli were cultured with CS and CS-CDs under the same conditions. Minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) values of 15 passages of CS and CS-CDs were detected, respectively (Fig. 2e). In CS incubation system, drug resistance of E. coli began to increase sharply at the 13th generation, and the increase was large. In CS-CDs incubation system, drug resistance of E. coli increased at the 13th passage and then decreased at the 14th passage. It demonstrated that the prepared CS-CDs not only retained part antibacterial activity of CS, but also weakened the bacterial resistance in a certain extent. Generally, bacteria secrete a large number of β-lactamases, which can adhere to β-lactam ring antibiotics, resulting in the inactivation of antibiotics. Therefore, after CS-CDs were formed, of which much β-lactam rings disappeared, and the antibacterial mechanism of CS-CDs partly changed. The effects of β-lactamase secreted by bacteria on the activity of CS-CDs was reduced, and the resistance of E. coli to CS-CDs was decreased.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Scanning electron microscope (SEM) were used to observe the morphological changes of E. coli after incubation with CS and CS-CDs under same condition for 0, 2, 6, 12 and 24 h, respectively (Fig. 3, a1-d5 ). In the TEM images: Cell walls of E. coli in both CS-CDs and CS groups at 0 h were smooth and clearly visible. The density of intracellular substances were uniform. At 2 h, cell walls of E. coli were relatively complete, and the damage was not obvious. With the prolonged incubation time (∼6h), structures of E. coli in the two groups were damaged more seriously. After incubation for 12 h and 24 h, the bacterial walls and membranes were obviously ruptured and intracellular substances leaked out. As in the SEM images (Fig. 3, c1-c5 and d1-d5), the destruction process of E. coli incubated with CS-CDs and CS was similar with the results in TEM images. It was worthy to note that both CS-CDs and CS drugs acted on the cell walls of bacteria to achieve antibacterial effects.

Bacterial biofilm is a protective film formed by the mucus secreted by bacteria, which can avoid or reduce the damage of bacteria from external environment. Considering the good antibacterial activity of CS-CDs, the antibiofilm effect was evaluated from the perspective of quantitative and qualitative analysis. Both CS-CDs and CS showed dose-dependent toxicity to E. coli biofilms (Fig. 3e, Fig. 3f and Supporting Information, Fig. S8). At the same concentration of 20 μg/mL, CS-CDs had little destructive effect on the bacterial biofilm, while CS had obvious destructive effect. When the concentration increased to 30 μg/mL, CS-CDs significantly damaged the bacterial biofilm, which was close to CS. When the concentration increased to 50 μg/mL and more, the destruction effect of CS-CDs on bacterial biofilm became better than that of CS. Compared with CS, CS-CDs showed higher anti-biofilm efficiency at higher concentration.

An acute E. coli BSI model was established with male ICR mice, to estimate therapeutic effect of the prepared CS-CDs. The mice were randomly divided into Model group, CS group and CS-CDs group. The mortality rate of mice in Model group was 100% within 24 h after establishment of acute E. coli BSI model. All relevant data of mice in Model group (including concentration of bacteria in the blood of mice, IL-6 index, number of white blood cells in mice body, mice body temperature, etc.) were obtained within 12 h after the establishment of BSI model (Fig. 4 , Supporting Information, Fig. S9 and Fig. S10). Referring to the clinical acute BSI treatment regimen of CS, the drug dose for mice in both CS group and CS-CDs group was 80 mg/kg once a day. On 1st d, 3rd d, 5th d and 7th days, the peripheral blood of mice in CS group and CS-CDs group were randomly selected for blood culture (Fig. 4a). On the first day, blood of mice in each group contained a large amount of E. coli. With continuous treatment, the amount of E. coli in blood of mice in CS group and CS-CDs group decreased significantly on the 3rd day. On the 5th day, there was still a small amount of E. coli in the two groups. On the 7th day, E. coli was basically undetectable in CS-CDs group, but still a little in CS group. As can be seen in the corresponding bacteria count plate statistic diagrams of mice in each group, on the 5th and 7th days, CS-CDs showed better bactericidal efficacy than CS (Fig. 4b).

1 h after the injection of bacterial solution, body temperature of mice in the Model group, CS group, and CS-CDs group began to drop (Supporting Information, Fig. S9) [39]. The body temperature of mice in Model group continued to drop until they died. And because their immune system was attacked by a large number of bacteria, the levels of IL-6 in the mice rose sharply. 3 h after drug injection, the body temperature of mice in CS group decreased to lowest of ∼ 31.3 °C, with motionless. It was probably because CS stimulated the mice body after injection and hindered the normal operation of their immune system. The level of IL-6 of mice in CS group also increased sharply, approaching that of mice in Model group (Fig. 4c). The body temperature of mice in CS-CDs group did not decrease dramatically. At the same time, although the expression of IL-6 of mice in CS-CDs group was also higher than that in Control group, it was much lower than that in Model group and CS group. It suggested that CS-CDs were less stimulating to living organisms than CS in the treatment of BSI. Also, compared with the mice in Control group, body temperature and IL-6 expression of mice in Control + CS-CDs group changed little (from 1st d to 7th d). It proved the injection of antibacterial drug CS-CDs exhibited little stimulation to mice body. At ∼ 24 h after drug injection, the body temperature of mice in CS group and CS-CDs group began to re-rise, indicating the acute symptoms began to be improved. As the treatment time extended to 7 days, the body temperature and IL-6 levels of mice in each group were basically the same, and only the body temperature of mice in CS group was slightly lower than that of other groups. The number of white blood cell in the peripheral blood of mice in each group was monitored (Fig. 4d, and Supporting Information, Fig. S10). One hour after BSI modeling, the number of white blood cell in Model group, CS group and CS-CDs group decreased clearly. The number of white blood cell in Model group continued to drop sharply before dying. At 3 h after administration, the number of white blood cell of mice in CS group decreased slightly, while that in CS-CDs group increased apparently. After 48 h, the number of white blood cell of mice in CS group and CS-CDs group gradually recovered, which was basically the same as that in Control group. In subsequent treatment, the number of white blood cell of mice in CS group and CS-CDs group was always higher than that in Control group. At 7th day, the number of white blood cell of mice in CS-CDs group was slightly higher than that in CS group. In the late stage of treatment, the number of bacteria in mice had dropped significantly. In order to prevent the bacteria from continuing to multiply, the injection volume of antibacterial drugs had not been reduced, which may have certain drug stimulation to the mice body. On the other hand, during the 7 days, compared with mice in Control group, the number of white blood cell of mice in Control + CS-CDs group changed slightly, with a little difference.

For the aim to evaluate the pathological damage of the drugs to mice during the treatment process, H&E staining was performed on the main organs (heart, lungs, spleen, liver and kidneys) of the mice in each group. Note that the mortality rate of mice in Model group within 24 h of injection bacteria was 100%. Therefore, the H&E staging images of mice in Model group were obtained on the 1st day of the experimental cycle in vivo. The H&E staging images of main organ of mice in other groups were obtained on the 7th day, at the end of the experimental cycle (Supporting Information, Fig. S11). The results of H&E staining sections showed that the Model group had obvious pathological characteristics of BSI. While after treatment, the relevant lesions in CS group and CS-CDs group were alleviated. The Control + CS-CDs group showed similar histological characteristics as the Control group, demonstrating the low biological toxicity of CS-CDs. See Fig. S11 for detailed histopathology descriptions of each group. The above experimental results proved that CS-CDs can safely and effectively treat acute BSI in mice.

Considering the binding effects between CS-CDs and penicillin-binding proteins/peptidoglycan in bacterial cell walls, lots of consumption of fluorescent CS-CDs may happened to promote bacterial lysis. Their nanostructure may be destroyed, leading to the decrease of fluorescence intensity. 6 μL anticoagulated blood from ICR mice, 1 mg/mL CS-CDs aqueous solution and E. coli suspension of increasing concentration (0.5 × 106, 2.0 × 106, 5.0 × 106, 1.0 × 107, 1.0 × 108, 2.0 × 108, 5.0 × 108, and 1.0 × 109 CFU/mL) were incubated together in a room environment for 6 h. It can be seen that under the same condition, the greater the concentration of bacteria, the darker the color of the mixed solution. Under the excitation of 365 nm UV light, fluorescence of the mixed solution became weaker to the increasing concentration of E. coli (Fig. 5 a). After incubation for 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 h, the fluorescence spectrum of each mixed solution under 390 nm light excitation was measured timely (Supporting Information, Fig. S12). Taking fluorescence intensity of each mixed solution (CS-CDs, blood, and E. coli) at each time point as ordinate and the number of E. coli as abscissa, their fitted function curves were obtained (Fig. 5b). After incubating for 1–2 h, fluorescence intensity of the mixed solutions in each group did not change much. It can be seen in the TEM and SEM images of Fig. 3, at the initial incubation period (0 ∼ 2 h), the morphology of E. coli cell walls changed little. It indicated that the connection effect of CS-CDs to the E. coli cell walls was weak. The consumption of CS-CDs was not much (Fig. 3a2 and Fig. 3c2), and the fluorescence intensity of all the co-incubation systems decreased slightly (Fig. 5b and Fig. S12). When incubation time was prolonged to 3–5 h, in the serials of mixed solution with the same concentration of CS-CDs, the fluorescence of the mixed solution decreased more clearly with the increasing concentration of bacteria in each solution. When incubated for 6 h, with the increase of the concentration of bacteria, fluorescence intensity of each mixed solution decreased most significantly. The fluorescent intensity exhibited quadratic functional relationship with the number of bacteria (Fig. 5c). It can be explained that at ∼ 6 h, structures of E. coli cell walls were damaged more seriously (Fig. 3a3 and Fig. 3c3). The higher the E. coli concentration, the more CS-CDs were consumed, and the fluorescence decrease signal of the system were more obvious (Fig. 5, Table 1 and Fig. S12). Thus, it is more conducive to detecting the concentration of E. coli at 6 h. The linear relationship between I0/I vs the different concentration of E. coli after coculturing with 1 mg/mL CS-CDs for 6 h. In the corresponding linear fitting diagram of fluorescence intensity vs different concentrations of E. coli after culturing with 1 mg/mL CS-CDs for 6 h, the detection concentration range for E. coli was 0.5 × 106 ∼ 1 × 109 CFU/mL, and the detection limit (LOD) was 3.7 × 105 CFU/mL (Supporting Information, Fig. S13).

Note that there were increased fluorescence intensity values of each mixed solution with time in Fig. S12. The fluorescence spectra of CS-CDs solution without any bacteria for 1–6 h confirmed that there was no fluorescence quenching or enhancement of CS-CDs solution throughout the experiment (Supporting Information, Fig. S14). Taking into account the possible interference of blood on the fluorescence system, fluorescence measurements were performed on mouse blood alone (Supporting Information, Fig. S15). The results showed the fluorescence intensity of blood was faint under the excitation of 390 nm light. Further, we demonstrated that the fluorescence (Ex = 390 nm, Em = 465 nm) of contents (released after bacterial fragmentation) was weak, too (Supporting Information, Fig. S16). We speculated that after incubation with CS-CDs, the contents released from E. coli (including enzymes, proteins, nucleic acids, etc.) may bind to CS-CDs through supramolecular interaction, such as hydrogen bonding [40]. It helped to enhance the rigidity of the CS-CDs structure, hinder their vibration and rotation, and promote the radiation relaxation processes, resulting in enhanced fluorescence in the mixed system [41]. As the incubation time extended, contents released more from E. coli after destruction, accompanied with a gradual increase in fluorescence intensity in the mixed system. It should be emphasized that although the fluorescence intensity of the mixed system gradually increased with time, when compared at the same incubation time (such as 6 h): the higher the bacterial concentration, the more luminescent CS-CDs were destroyed, and the lower the fluorescence intensity of the mixed solution. From Fig. 5, Fig. S12 and Fig. S13, it can be seen that for the mixed system, the response relationship between the decrease in fluorescence intensity and the increase in bacterial count was affected little. Moreover, increasing the fluorescence signal of the system may be more conducive to signal capture and detection.

In addition, we adopted the same experimental conditions to estimate the fluorescence quantitative detection ability of CS-CDs on other bacteria, such as K. pneumoniae, S. aureus, and P. aeruginosa (Supporting Information, Fig. S17, Fig. S18 and Fig. S19). The results showed that the above three bacteria cannot be quantitatively detected by changing the fluorescence intensity of CS-CDs. We further applied the same quantification system for another two kinds of E. coli bacteria strain (E. coli CFT073, and E. coli DH5α) (Supporting Information, Fig. S20 and Fig. S21). Similarly, there was linear relationship between I0/I vs the different concentration of E. coli CFT073, and E. coli DH5α after coculturing with 1 mg/mL CS-CDs after 6 h. It suggested this quantitative detection method was specific for E. coli.

In conclusion, CS-CDs were rapidly synthesized by one-step microwave method using antibiotic CS as sole precursor. Through characterization analysis, CS-CDs retained part of the structure and properties of CS. Therefore, CS-CDs had low biological toxicity and good antibacterial activity in vitro and in vivo. An acute E. coli BSI model of ICR mice was established and the results suggested CS-CDs could be used to cure acute BSI effectively in vivo. Compared with CS in body temperature, IL-6 and white blood cell count, CS-CDs had less stimulation and a better healing effect for mice. What was more, under the same conditions, the higher the bacteria concentration, the lower the fluorescence intensity of the bacteriostatic system with CS-CDs. The CS-CDs synthesized here achieved the dual purpose of diagnosis and synergistic treatment of BSI. Using bacteria-sensitive antibiotic molecules to prepare CDs, on the basis of retaining the bacteriostatic effect of antibiotics, the fluorescence properties of CDs can be used to monitor the concentration of bacteria in organism. It should point out in this study the CS-CDs fluorescence decreasing with the increasing of bacteria concentration was not ideal. It may be due to the fact that CS was the only precursor, which was aim to minimize the biological toxicity of prepared CS-CDs. In the future, for the purpose to advance the effect of fluorescence monitoring, it can be considered to introduce brighter and more sensitive fluorescent agents into the antibiotic carbon nanodots system. It will improve the clinical application value of this technology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jilin Province (YDZJ202201ZYTS562), Jilin Scientific and Technological Development Program (20210204176YY, 20140622003JC and 2021Z003).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.143988.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An insight into the potentials of carbon dots for in vitro live-cell imaging: recent progress, challenges, and prospects

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Hallaji, Z. Bagheri, M. Oroujlo, M. Nemati, Z. Tavassoli, B. Ranjbar, An insight into the potentials of carbon dots for in vitro live-cell imaging: recent progress, challenges, and prospects, Chem. Soc. Rev. 189 (2022) 190.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step synthesis of amikacin modified fluorescent carbon dots for the detection of Gram-negative bacteria like Escherichia coli
